text: We have the pleasure to present to you today a pancreatectomy made totally by laparoscopy with a patient of eighty-one years old. It was presented with non-fluctuant obstructive anicter with a bilirubin of eight milligrams per deciliter. Different examinations were carried out as a scan, an echoendoscopy. And later, once we saw that it was recyclable, a preoperative ERCP to decrease the ICTER having progressed. The scanner shows us the dilatation of the VIRSUNG and the coendoscopy. It shows us the tumor. These images of the scanner also show us that there are no compromises with the vessel.

The surgeon positions the trocars to access the abdominal cavity, beginning with the lowering of the hepatic flexure of the colon. Extreme cauterization is performed to highlight the inferior vena cava, the confluence with the right renal vein, and the aorta. The dissection proceeds leftward to reach the peritoneum, releasing the duodenum. The common hepatic artery is exposed with ganglionary dissection, followed by similar dissection at the gastro-duodenal artery. The liver is sectioned distally, allowing access to the pancreas. The transverse mesocolon is lifted to expose the superior mesenteric vein. The pancreas is then suspended and sectioned distally using an ultrasonic scalpel, with the pancreatic duct (Virsung) measuring three millimeters, secured with a percutaneous approach.

The surgeon sections the first range of the Treitz ligament to control the gastro-duodenal artery, which is then sectioned between security clips. The right edge of the superior mesenteric vein and the portal vein are delicately released by pulling the medial part towards the right lateral. Collaterals of the portal vein are identified and sectioned without clips. The back portal blade is removed from the upper mesenteric artery. A clamp is placed at the gallbladder, which is sectioned, and the tutor is also sectioned. The gallbladder is dissected and sectioned at the hepatic canal level, followed by the removal of the titer drain tips, completing the operation with a cholecystectomy.

The surgeon performs a pancreatojejunostomy, creating a lateral terminal anastomosis using barbed wire sutures. The posterior wall of the anastomosis is completed, with the Virsung canal supported in the laparoscopic view. An anterotomy is made using a monopolar bisturi, and the drenthutter is passed into the jejunum. The anterior plan is then constructed with the same sutures, securing the Virsung canal to the jejunum.

The surgeon completes the suture, preparing to perform the gynecological hepatitis anastomosis approximately twenty to twenty-five centimeters further using PDS 4-0 sutures. The posterior sutures are placed first, followed by the anterior sutures, with a lost drain inserted to secure the anastomosis. The surgeon is positioned between the patient's legs for optimal access during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the hepatic gastrointestinal anastomosis using the last thread. Approximately seventy centimeters from the previous anastomoses, the site for the gastrointestinal anastomosis is selected and cleaved using a linear clamp for a wide lateral anastomosis to facilitate stomach emptying. The anterotomy orifices are closed with barbed wire sutures. Two drains are positioned to cover the pancreatic and hepatico-jejunal anastomoses. The surgical specimens are placed at the pelvis level for extraction through a pharyngeal incision.
